tpIctyqH29Q.0: Hello world, I'm Carrie-Anne Philbin and welcome to Crash Course Computer Science.
tpIctyqH29Q.1: So, computers really have allowed us to do some pretty amazing things. Think global telecommunications,
tpIctyqH29Q.2: international commerce, global transportation, breakthroughs in medicine, distributed education,
tpIctyqH29Q.3: online shopping, online dating, and just the internet in general.
tpIctyqH29Q.4: Computers are allowing us to explore our own world and other worlds, and of course some
tpIctyqH29Q.5: seemingly mundane things like permitting us to spawn our pets from work or communicating
tpIctyqH29Q.6: with our friends in a nearly indecipherable stream of emoji. But don't call computers
tpIctyqH29Q.7: magical. They are not. I repeat, are not magical. So before we get into what we're going to talk
tpIctyqH29Q.8: about in this series, it might be useful to tell you what we are not going to talk about.
tpIctyqH29Q.9: We aren't going to teach you how to program. Programming is a really crucial aspect of
tpIctyqH29Q.10: computer science, and we will get to the rules that guide the logic of hardware and software
tpIctyqH29Q.11: design. But we aren't going to teach you how to program an Arduino to water your plant,
tpIctyqH29Q.12: or how to change the CSS on your grandma's sewing blog so visitors' curses...
